crazy_prediction.text: {"name":"Two or more drivers crash into the Wall of Champions across the weekend","reasoning":"With a 60-70% chance of rain on Sunday, cooler-than-usual temperatures, only one hour of practice due to the Sprint format, and the entirely new 2026 cars that drivers admit they're scared to drive in the wet (per Brundle), the infamous Wall of Champions is primed for multiple victims. Limited track time + new low-downforce cars + wet conditions = chaos at the final chicane."}

flop.text: {"name":"Ferrari","reasoning":"Ferrari has been fast in qualifying with great starts but consistently fades during races. Montreal's heavy braking zones, tyre management demands and threat of mixed conditions expose their race-pace weakness. With Hamilton historically strong here but the SF-26 inconsistent, expect Ferrari to qualify well but underperform on Sunday — especially if rain throws strategy into chaos."}

good_surprise.text: {"name":"Hadjar","reasoning":"Hadjar has been the standout performer in his rookie-to-sophomore Red Bull seat, with Red Bull praising his pure speed and adaptability. Montreal's stop-start nature and low-downforce setup tend to favour brave, precise drivers. With potential rain on Sunday (60-70% chance), a wet race adds unpredictability that could let Hadjar exploit Red Bull's improving package and his street-circuit confidence to grab a surprise top-5."}

flop.text: {"name":"Aston Martin","reasoning":"Aston Martin was the only team not to bring upgrades to Miami, and they may again arrive in Canada with a blank upgrade sheet. good_surprise.text: {"name":"Sainz","reasoning":"Carlos Sainz has finished in the points in two of the last three races, showing increasing comfort with the Williams 2026 car. With rain forecast and a high likelihood of safety cars, Sainz's consistency could deliver a surprise top‑6 finish, well above expectations."}

crazy_prediction.text: {"name":"A rain-triggered red flag before lap 10","reasoning":"Sunday’s forecast points to a significant rain chance, and Montreal already has close walls, cold tyres, heavy braking zones, and a historically high Safety Car profile. With the grid likely set in dry conditions but the race potentially starting in changing weather, the opening laps could be especially treacherous. A multi-car incident or a car buried at the final chicane early on is unlikely but very plausible."}

flop.text: {"name":"Mercedes","reasoning":"Mercedes are the obvious benchmark right now, with Antonelli and Russell dominating the early-season narrative and the market expecting them to control Canada. That makes them vulnerable as a flop pick: anything short of a win or double podium will feel like underperformance. Montreal’s Sprint format limits setup time, cooler temperatures could make tyre warm-up tricky, and Sunday’s rain risk increases the chance of strategy disruption. I think their pace will still be real, but the weekend may be too chaotic for a clean Mercedes conversion."}

good_surprise.text: {"name":"Cadillac","reasoning":"Expectations are still low because Cadillac entered Canada near the bottom of the constructors' picture, but Montreal is exactly the kind of circuit where an outsider can overdeliver: heavy braking, long straights, possible rain on Sunday, Safety Car risk, and only one practice session before parc fermé. The team reportedly arrived trying to build on its Miami upgrade momentum, and a cool, variable weekend should reward experienced race management from Bottas and Perez. I’m backing Cadillac to look much more competitive than its baseline pace suggests and sneak into the points if the race gets messy."}
